one_line_takeaway: Casper shows that a fully client-side, three-layer (regex + BERT-NER + local Llama-3-8B-over-WebGPU) browser extension can redact PII and flag sensitive topics in ChatGPT prompts at 98.5% PII / ~90% topic accuracy for ~15% latency overhead, without any change to the LLM service.
key_finding: The three layers complement each other and run cheaply enough for a consumer laptop. NER achieves 98.5% true-positive detection of PII-bearing prompts. Llama 3 identifies medical topics at 96.6% and legal topics at 83.2% (the abstract's headline 89.9% topic accuracy is the average of these two). Model choice and query structure matter sharply: Llama 3's medical detection rate is 29.8 percentage points above Llama 2 (96.6% vs 66.8%), and asking one topic per query yields 96.6% vs only 32.4% when multiple topics are combined in a single query. End-to-end, all three stages add roughly 14.7% overhead (<2.5s) to a ~15-token ChatGPT interaction, with memory dominated by the ~6.15GB Llama 3 8B model.
core_contribution: Casper is a client-side prompt-sanitization system, implemented as a ~1000-line TypeScript Google Chrome extension (targeting ChatGPT's web interface, Chrome v124), that detects and redacts privacy-sensitive information from user prompts before they are sent to a cloud LLM service, with zero modification to the LLM service. Its core is a three-layered pipeline run entirely on the user's device: (1) a rule-based regex/keyword filter (10 built-in PII patterns, user-extensible), (2) an ML named-entity recognizer (bert-base-NER, 110M params, loaded in-browser via Transformers.js), and (3) a WebGPU-accelerated local LLM topic identifier (Llama 3 8B via the WebLLM framework) that flags sensitive topics carrying no explicit PII. PIIs are replaced with reversible Faker.js pseudo-placeholders (mapping kept in browser local storage) so responses can be reverted to the real values, while sensitive topics trigger a user warning. The authors also present an empirical study of third-party ChatGPT plugin privacy policies (Table 1) motivating the need. The paper claims Casper is the first client-side system addressing privacy in the LLM web interface.
threat_model: Users are honest and use an uncompromised system/browser, and are willing to share prompts with LLM providers and certain third-party plugins, but do not want to entrust those providers with protecting their sensitive information. LLM service providers and third-party plugin providers are trusted only to correctly fulfill requests and to use encrypted channels/standard access controls, but are NOT trusted to withstand attacks: they may collect/retain prompts for research and business purposes, and sensitive data can be exposed via server-side data breaches or ML model-inversion attacks. Third-party plugins are considered higher-risk than the LLM provider due to varied/weaker privacy practices. The browser and operating system are trusted (Casper does not rely on trusted/confidential computing). The design goal is that raw sensitive prompt content never leaves the user's device.
limitations: (1) English-only prototype (design claimed extensible but not demonstrated). (2) All evaluation is on synthetic GPT-4-Turbo-generated prompts, not real user data; ground truth comes from the same generating query, and prompt-generation vs detection being two different models drives both false positives and negatives. (3) Only two sensitive-topic categories (medical, legal) tested; legal detection is notably weaker at 83.2% (16.8% false negatives). (4) NER false-positive rate is high at 13.3% (e.g. country names like 'France'), tolerated only because placeholders are transparently reverted. (5) Chrome extension API cannot intercept the prompt at send time, so Casper relies on the user pressing Control-Enter to trigger sanitization (or can only block, not modify, on submission) — a user who forgets bypasses protection. (6) Multi-topic degradation forces sequential per-topic queries, increasing latency. (7) The ~6.15GB local-LLM memory footprint requires reasonably capable hardware; longer prompts (1000 tokens) take up to ~22.5s. (8) No comparison against an external competing system — evaluation is internal (Llama 2 vs Llama 3, straw-man design alternatives).

datasets_used: NER set: 1000 prompts with named entities + 1000 without
Topic set: 1000 topic-bearing (500 medical + 500 legal) + 1000 neutral prompts
CoNLL-2003 NER dataset (training data of bert-base-NER: 6600 persons, 7140 locations, 6321 organizations, 3438 miscellaneous)
Table 1 study: 11 third-party ChatGPT plugins' privacy policies (Expedia, FiscalNote, Instacart, KAYAK, Klarna, Milo, OpenTable, Shopify, Slack, Wolfram, Zapier)
quantitative_results: NER PII detection: 98.5% true positive, 1.5% false negative; 13.3% false positive, 86.7% true negative (Table 3)
Medical topic ID (Llama 3): 96.6% TP, 3.4% FN, 4.2% FP, 95.8% TN (Table 4)
Legal topic ID (Llama 3): 83.2% TP, 16.8% FN, 5.8% FP, 94.2% TN (Table 5)
Headline topic accuracy 89.9% = average of 96.6% (medical) and 83.2% (legal)
Llama 3 medical detection 29.8 pts higher than Llama 2 (96.6% vs 66.8%)
Single-topic vs multi-topic query accuracy: 96.6% vs 32.4% (Table 2)
End-to-end overhead: <2.5s for ~15-token prompt = 14.7% of ChatGPT response time; up to ~22.5s for 1000-token prompt
Per-stage time (15 tokens): rule-based ~10ms, NER ~0.2s, topic ID ~2.15s
Resource: ~6.15GB memory (Llama 3 8B model), ~20% CPU on 10-core machine, GPU-bound
Local inference speed: Llama 2 7B 12.88 tok/s, Llama 3 7B 10.52 tok/s, TinyLlama 1.1B 50.73 tok/s
Plugin study: all 11 plugins share data with partners; only 27.3% of policies mention LLM/generative AI
Implementation: ~1000 lines of TypeScript; 10 built-in regex PII patterns; NER default confidence threshold 90/100
ChatGPT baseline response time (Table 6): total ranges ~7.25s (7-9PM) to ~17s (9-11AM medical)
baselines_compared: Llama 2 vs Llama 3 as the local topic-identification model (66.8% vs 96.6% medical)
Single-topic vs multiple-topic query formulation (96.6% vs 32.4%)
ChatGPT round-trip response time with vs without Casper (overhead baseline)
Conceptual straw-man alternatives argued against: full local LLM inference, and cloud/server-side PII redaction
No external competing sanitization system evaluated head-to-head
